content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 10, 2018

Keith F. Collins
Jones & Mayer
3777 N. Harbor Blvd
Fullerton, CA 92835

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-183

Dear Mr. Collins:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Whittier City Councilmember Josue Alvarado regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Alvarado from taking part in decisions relating to an ordinance that would impose "enhanced operating standards" on recycling facilities, including a prohibition on any recycling facility being located within 200 feet of any residential use, given that the Councilmember's residence is located 300 feet from an existing recycling facility that would be subject to that prohibition if the ordinance is adopted?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest in his residence.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR

Your firm serves as City Attorney for the City of Whittier, and you are the authorized representative of Councilmember Alvarado. The Councilmember owns and resides in residential

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-183
Page No. 2

real property within the City. You state that the Councilmember's residence is located 300 feet from
an existing recycling facility. That recycling facility is less than 200 feet from multiple other
residential real properties.

The City will soon consider whether to adopt an ordinance that would impose "enhanced
operating standards" on recycling facilities. Among other things, the ordinance would:

• Prohibit any recycling facility from being located within 200 feet of any residential use.

• Establish a five-year amortization period during which a recycling facility in existence prior to
the adoption of the ordinance would be authorized to continue operating at its present location
despite that recycling facility being a nonconforming use, so long as the recycling facility does
not expand its operations or increase its nonconformance with the ordinance.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or attempting to
use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that would have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) Interests that
may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103. Of
those interests, the facts presented indicate that the Councilmember has the following interests in
the decisions relating to the ordinance:

• A real property interest in his residence assuming he has a direct or indirect interest in his
residence worth $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

• An interest in his personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See Section
87103.)

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701 sets forth the foreseeability standards applicable to a decision's effect on
an official's interest. A decision's effect on an official's interest is presumed to be reasonably
foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and an official's interest is explicitly
involved if it is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) With
respect to an official's interest that is not explicitly involved, the decision's effect is reasonably
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or
theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) Neither the Councilmember's real property interest in his
residence nor his interest in his personal finances is explicitly involved in the decisions relating to
the ordinance.

Regulation 18702.2 provides the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an
official's real property interest. Paragraph (11) of subdivision (a) of that regulation, the Act's 500-
foot rule, provides that a decision's effect on an official's residential real property interest is
material if the decision will affect the value of other real property located within 500 feet of the
official's property, unless there are sufficient facts to indicate that the decision will not have a

File No. A-18-183
Page No. 3

reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's property. If adopted, the ordinance
would prohibit the existing recycling facility located 300 feet from the Councilmember's residence
from continuing to operate in its current location, beyond the ordinance's five-year amortization
period, because that location is less than 200 feet from multiple other residential real properties.
Therefore, the decisions relating to the ordinance would have a reasonably foreseeable measurable
impact on the Councilmember's real property interest in his residence.

Thus, the Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest in his
residence.²

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

[illegible]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

_________________________

² We do not further analyze the effect of the decisions relating to the ordinance on the Councilmember's
interest in his personal finances and those of immediate family members because we have already determined that those
decisions would have a disqualifying effect on his real property interest in his residence.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Alvarado from taking part in decisions relating to an 
ordinance that would impose "enhanced operating standards" on recycling facilities, including a 
prohibition on any recycling facility being located within 200 feet of any residential use, given that 
the Councilmember's residence is located 300 feet from an existing recycling facility that would be 
subject to that prohibition if the ordinance is adopted?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they 
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest in his 
residence.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Alvarado from taking part in decisions relating to an 
ordinance that would impose "enhanced operating standards" on recycling facilities, including a 
prohibition on any recycling facility being located within 200 feet of any residential use, given that 
the Councilmember's residence is located 300 feet from an existing recycling facility that would be 
subject to that prohibition if the ordinance is adopted?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they 
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest in his 
residence.
